steps.content: A class is a blueprint for creating objects. Think of it like a cookie cutter — the class defines the shape, and each object (or instance) is a cookie made from that cutter. Classes bundle together data (attributes) and behavior (methods) into a single package.

The class keyword defines a class. Inside, we write methods — functions that belong to the class. To create an instance, we call the class like a function: Dog(). Then we call methods on that instance with the dot notation: my_dog.bark().

The __init__ method is the constructor. It runs automatically when you create a new instance and is where you set up initial attributes. Think of it as the setup instructions that run every time you stamp out a new object.
The self parameter refers to the current instance. When you call my_dog.bark(), Python automatically passes my_dog as the self argument. That's how the method knows which instance's data to use.

Instance methods are regular functions inside a class. Their first parameter is always self, which gives them access to the instance's attributes and other methods. You can define as many as you need to model the behavior of your objects.
The real power of classes is creating many independent instances. Each one has its own set of attributes — changing one doesn't affect the others.
Each instance is completely separate. dog1.name and dog2.name are different values stored in different memory locations. Classes give you a clean way to organize code that works with structured data.
